items.q: When do you use the imperfect rather than the passé composé?
items.correct: For ongoing, habitual, or descriptive past actions.
items.random_wrong: When you don't know what tense to use.
items.why: Students confuse past tenses by recency. The distinction is ASPECTUAL: passé composé = completed event ('I went'); imparfait = ongoing/habitual/descriptive ('I used to go', 'I was going').

items.q: How do you say 'I do not eat' in French?
items.why: Students forget the verb 'sandwich'. Ne and pas wrap AROUND the verb in present tense - ne BEFORE the verb, pas AFTER. In compound tenses, they wrap around the auxiliary.

items.q: How do you say 'I study maths' in French?
items.why: Students drop the article like English. School subjects in French take the DEFINITE article: 'j'aime le français', 'j'étudie les sciences', 'je déteste l'histoire'. This is the rule for general nouns.

items.q: Monet is associated with which movement?
items.why: Correct answer: Impressionnisme. “Cubisme” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
Correct answer: Masterpiece. “Workshop” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
Exact answer: 1874. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
